posts.text:
I have been part of more than 1000+ interviews (500+ at amazon and ~500 in different settings) 
I'm going to share some of the most important things which I learned from these interviews. 

🧩 Crack the System Design Interview — Part 1 of 20

What Interviewers Are Actually Scoring You On 
Most engineers walk into a system design interview believing one thing:
"If I get the architecture right, I pass."
I used to think the same thing - that belief alone has cost some very talented engineers the job.
Here's the truth nobody tells you upfront.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The interview isn't about the perfect design.
There is no perfect design. Every system has trade-offs. Every architecture decision you make solves one problem and creates another. Interviewers know this. What they're really watching is how you think, not what you know.
I've seen engineers sketch a flawless distributed system and still fail. I've also seen engineers with a messy whiteboard get the offer. The difference wasn't talent. It was how they communicated.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
 Here's what's actually being evaluated:
- Problem Clarity Do you ask the right questions before jumping in? Or do you assume you already know what needs to be built?
- Structured Thinking Is your approach organized and easy to follow? Can the interviewer see where you're going?
- Trade-off Awareness Do you acknowledge what you're giving up with each decision? Every choice has a cost. Senior engineers name it.
- Communication Can you explain your thinking clearly while you're still figuring things out? This one is underrated and it matters enormously.
- Adaptability When the interviewer pushes back or changes a constraint, do you adapt — or do you freeze?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The pattern I see most often:
An engineer spends 40 minutes building a beautiful system. Never once explains why they made a choice. Never asks a clarifying question. And when the interviewer says "what if we needed 10x the traffic?" — they fall apart.

That's not a knowledge problem. That's a communication problem.

The best candidates I've seen treat the interview like a live problem-solving session with a colleague. They think out loud. They say things like "I'm going with a relational database here because consistency matters more than scale for this use case — but I'd revisit that if we hit 50 million users." That one sentence tells me more than a perfect diagram ever could.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The three questions every interviewer is quietly asking themselves:
❓ Would I want to work through a hard problem with this person? 
❓ Do they stay clear and calm under pressure? 
❓ Do they know what they don't know — and are they honest about it?

If you can make an interviewer answer yes to all three, the technical details almost take care of themselves.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
#SystemDesign 

Note: Used Linkedin AI to structure the post - data is coming from my notes.

posts.text:
Every fitness tip I have from 14 years in the gym:

1. Cut your alcohol intake

2. Don't drink your calories.

3.  Fitness is 90% mental and 10% physical.

4. Morning workouts are a great way to start the day.

5. Carbs don't make you fat and fats don't make you fat - too many calories do.

6. The only equipment you need is your own body.

7. Lifting weights is better than cardio for fat loss.

8. If you aren't losing weight, you are eating too much.

9.  If you aren't gaining weight, you aren't eating enough.

10. Increase weight and reps every workout to build muscle.

11. You aren't hungry, you're just bored.

12. Coffee + sun is the ultimate AM energy hack.

13. Women don't want visible abs, they want visible confidence.

14. Hydration is key for great workouts.

15. Building more muscle is the best fat burner.

16. Abs are built in the gym and revealed in the kitchen.

17. For more muscle = 6-20 reps

18. For strong muscle =1-6 reps

19. Keto is for losers (unless you're epileptic).

20. Track your workouts to stay motivated.

21. More protein is key = Eat 1 gram per lb of lean body weight daily

22. You can get "instant results" after 3 months of doing the work.

23. 99% of exercises on IG are B.S.

24. Sodium is necessary to live, you can't eliminate it.

25. Getting super shredded boosts your confidence like completing an ultra marathon.

26. Staying super shredded can be unhealthy like a marathon that never ends.

27. Staying 12-15% body fat is ideal for men.

28. Discipline = Freedom

29. There will be bumps in the road, progress is never linear.

30. Have protein with every meal.

31. Eat your protein first and you're less likely to overeat.

32. Go for a walk outdoors, every day.

33. Expert accountability and a solid plan guarantee success.

34. You will never regret doing a workout you didn't want to do.

35. High-quality protein powder and eggs are less work than chewing meat.

36. Meat keeps you fuller longer.

37. You can get more out of a 30-minute workout by training to failure on every set.

38. Your last rep should be your slowest rep.

39. Man boobs are not sexy.

40. Eat like shit = Feel like shit

41. Comparing yourself to others is making you anxious - focus on yourself.

42. Rest days = Playing with kids, Nature park dates, Walking in the sun

43. Cardio is great for mental and cardiovascular health.

44. Cardio is shit for fat loss.

45. Exercise is literally a documented anti-depressant. Get in the gym.

46. Not keeping junk food and alcohol in your pantry is self-care.

47. Filling your home with nutrient-dense, protein-rich, and belly-filling foods is ultimate self-care.

48. Don't hang with unhealthy people.

What are your thoughts on this?

Let me know below.

& Thanks for reading!

If you enjoyed this post...

And follow me @WarrenEnglish_  for more content like this.

Some helpful points here — but there’s also misinformation that could be harmful. Promoting eggs, meat, and dairy as ideal protein sources ignores decades of science showing these foods increase the risk of heart disease, cancer, and all-cause mortality.
📌 The most nutrient-dense, anti-inflammatory, and longevity-supporting proteins are plant-based — lentils, beans, tofu, tempeh, seitan, quinoa, hemp, and chia:
 https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3662288/
📌 Whole food plant-based diets have been shown to reverse heart disease, reduce cancer risk, and support optimal body composition and mental health — without the IGF-1 spikes, saturated fat, cholesterol, or heme iron found in animal protein:
 https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5466931/
📌 Animal products don’t keep you full longer — fiber does. And plant foods come with antioxidants and phytonutrients that animal products lack entirely.
 https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3075924/
If we’re serious about health, we need to stop acting like meat and dairy are fitness foods. They're linked to disease — not longevity. A plant-based diet isn’t just sufficient for fitness goals — it’s optimal.

posts.text:
3 ways to get into product management without doing an MBA!

I often get asked if pursuing an MBA is necessary to break into product management and the short answer is NO!

An MBA can help by giving you a deep understanding of business concepts and strategies, a strong network, and job placement opportunities. But it could take 2 years and cost more than $200K to get an MBA from a top tier program.

The good news is that there are several ways to break into product management, and MBA is just one of them. Here are a few more options:

✅ Transitioning into a PM role in your current company: Great option if you’re already working in a tech company, have a stellar performance history, and have some experience with product development. 

Tips:
🎯 Enquire about formal PM rotations in your company
🎯 Raise your hand for stretch PM assignments
🎯 Network with your current colleagues and managers, and discuss your product management aspirations and readiness.

✅ APM programs: APM (Associate Product Manager) programs are highly competitive 2–3 year programs where the company provides you with on the job training and mentoring you need to become a successful product manager. 

Tips:
🎯 Research the different programs, and tailor your applications to each program. 
🎯 Pay attention to application deadlines
🎯 Doing side projects and highlighting transferable skills on your resume would strengthen your candidacy for such programs.

✅ Applying for regular PM jobs: You don't have to go back to school in this option, but it is still a lot of work and it could be difficult to get your foot in the door without any experience. 

Tips
🎯 Proactively network with people in the field and be ready to talk about your skills and experience
🎯 Build your portfolio of side projects that demonstrate PM skills
🎯 Take on volunteer work that can help develop and showcase PM skills and abilities.

Whichever route you choose, the key is to stay focused on your goals, develop relevant skills, and build relationships in the field.

Add your tips in comments, ask me more questions and don't forget to share this with someone who can benefit from this post!

#productmanagement #mbajobs #apm #entryway #careerguide

posts.text:
If you're going to mock interview

Here's what you need to know

First stop mock interviewing 

• To memorize answers
• To learn data structures
• To get a company referral

Memorizing the answer to 1000+ possible questions won't help you.

You have a lot of time to learn data structures on your own.

Not everyone is comfortable giving a referral to their workplace.

Instead, use mock interviews to practice

• Thinking out loud
• Pointing out edge cases
• Outlining your algorithm
• Communicating effectively
• Asking clarifying questions
• Proactively testing your code
• Handling interview stress and anxiety
• Discussing multiple solution tradeoffs

Whether I was at Microsoft, Amazon, or Google. These topics were always part of the interviewing rubric.

Most interviewees do well on coding.

You can take it a step further doing well on communicating.

Good luck.

#softwareengineer
#coding
#alexcancode

posts.text:
“Leadership is a privilege...”

My next guest on The Path is Microsoft CEO, Satya Nadella. Satya has spent the last 30 years of his career working his way through the company. But becoming CEO was never his goal. 

As a young engineer, Satya was drawn to Microsoft in 1992 by the opportunity to create something of value. He wanted everyone to be able to feel the freedom to express themselves through computing. posts.text:
Super pumped for Day 1 with AWS!
Its been an intense 3 years for me, but also an incredibly transformative time too. What began as an incredibly high stakes decision back in 2019 when I switched majors from MSEE to MSCS has finally paid off. I have been through so many job rejections that I’ve lost count.

In this post, I wanted to share my 2 cents about some major takeaways during my job hunt that I hope helps other job seekers in some way. 

There is one thing that I learned during this phase: you may face multiple rejections and it will most definitely hurt your motivation. But after a few days, you will recover, get over it, and keep going, so have faith in patience as it is the ultimate virtue in life! 

Do not get bogged down by rejections, pat yourself for a great attempt, figure out ways to enjoy your time through all this and practice strong mental health in any way you like (for me it was pranayam, gyming, cracking lame jokes with friends, traveling, gaming and playing my guitar) and trust that this rejection was necessary to set you up for the next attempt. Something that helped me reconcile with a rejection is a saying I heard a while back: “when competing for a job, you are essentially setup for failure”. You have no control over the outcome, you can only take pride in giving your best. In case of software interviews: prepare, execute, fail, identify MISTAKES, modify, repeat. It was necessary to fail, at least for me. 

I was terrible at data structures, algorithms and problem solving, and still have so much to learn, so to all my connections who are still hustling, keep hustling! I would be remiss not to give credit to all the LEGENDary friends (you know exactly who you all are) and family who helped me prepare and mentored me through the process. Kudos to Simran Bathla for a smooth onboarding and honestly being super dedicated at helping me click with the right team.

Cheers to new challenges, struggles and a ton of epic stuff to look forward to!

#amazon #aws #softwareengineer #newjob #newopportunities #newchallenges

Dear LinkedIn family, I need your love, compassion, and generosity to help save my life. Unfortunately, for almost two years now, I’ve been privately fighting a very rare and difficult to treat cancer. But even after beating it twice, it has relapsed, and we’ve exhausted all the treatment options available in India. My oncologists have suggested me to urgently get to the US for further treatment where newer treatment options are available. However, the costs of these treatments are beyond the means of any average Indian family, and I need your help to raise the funds needed to get these treatments. Time is of grave essence as I’m deteriorating fast, and it is a matter of life and death for me. I request you to help us in any way possible as time is of grave essence. You can find further details on the crowdfunding page.

Institutes such as Harvard Medical School are researching newer treatments for my condition, including possible curative options. However, for an unsponsored international patient like me, the costs are prohibitively high, and we cannot afford this treatment without your help. For context, an average Indian’s annual salary would only be able to cover a single day (~$10,000) in the hospital let alone any treatment. All treatment costs, including those for any complications caused by these new treatments need to be paid by the patient and are collected in advance. My parents have already staked all their savings in this fight, and we need your help to make sure I do not lose this fight due to a lack of funds.

I request you to help us in any way possible, through contributions or just by just sharing this to someone who might be able to contribute to my medical treatment. Would also request you to further share this in your networks to help me raise the funds needed for my treatment. Every like and share matters and no contribution is too small. My life is at stake, and I’ll be forever grateful for your support in this difficult time. This will always be a social debt upon me which I will try my best to repay. 

Thank you and please find all further details on the fundraiser page. In case you have any further questions or suggestions, please feel free to reach me on sarthakfightscancer[at]gmail[dot]com and I will try to reply at the earliest.

Just finished the course “Learning React.js”! #reactjs
Made my first web app UI with React.

Look out for these key concepts:
Creating and rendering React elements,
Representing UI elements with React components,
adding props to display dynamic data,
Building components with JS class,
Using React states to re-render and change state of components,
bind event functions to UI elements with state variables,
and Conditional rendering of components.

JSX is a boon! Makes writing your own components much easier and makes my source much cleaner.

Eve Porcello does a great job of breaking down React concepts, and a hands-on example to help you build on your own.

Deloitte's 2019 Global #Blockchain Survey was an interesting read.

Some key highlights:
~ 53% view blockchain as a critical priority. 
~ 83% believe there is a compelling business case for blockchain.
~ 56% view blockchain as a disruptor in their industry.
~ 43% believe that blockchain is overhyped. 

As part of a company that is constantly engaged with enterprise clients from various industry verticals, the stats above are quite realistic. 

Those who understand the business use case acknowledge blockchain as a disruptor and thus are able to prioritize it while those that feel it is overhyped are mostly missing the bigger picture.

I guess sometimes you need to experience the fall in order to know where you really stand?

#enterpriseblockchain #supplychain

comments.text: The study is welcome. However, it should not be viewed as a discovery, at least not qualitatively. The tremendous energy requirement (which therefore predicts the carbon footprint) forecasted for AI compute has been generally understood for more than a decade (or two), where conquest of the seemingly intractable problem relies on timely technology advances generally on pace with Moore’s Law; with the energy efficiency of the human brain (as the target) providing the “gravitational pull”.  This MIT study quantifies the tip of the iceberg. The problem tends a thousand times bigger. So, we might need to stay well aware if it. The energy efficiency of the human brain offers hope and increasingly more clues to researchers, but no guarantees. Unlike the law of Universal Gravitation, Moore’s Law is predicated to fail. There’s a lot to do (— so stay busy mi amigos y amigas).

resharedPost.text:
#DataScientist jobs are consistently ranked as the most in demand role across North America and Europe. The reality is that with data increasing everyday and more been made today than the previous 20 years combined this will continue. However there are 3 mistakes that I encounter everyday meeting with Data Scientists at all levels.
1. Outcome focused - just focused on coming up with the right solution, not spending enough time asking better questions and being a detective to see if they are answering the right question. Context is everything, important to understand how the systems work together and interact with the solution.
2. Fixated on the  #Algorithms. They can apply the latest #deeplearning techniques but dont understand how to extract simple patterns from the data. In some cases a decision tree or linear regression is better to use.
3. Ineffective Storyteller. Dump the information at the users, fail to find the pertinent points and why it matters to the users, not to mention that the vast majority of business users have no knowledge of understanding analytics.

In our podcast #AIMentors leaders in AI share their journey in datascience and offer advice to grow and develop your skills in #Datascience and how to overcome these problems.
What's your thoughts?

comments.text:
Totally agree! 
For me, the main problem is that in many cases neither the Data Scientist nor the organization leaders really know the best way to get value from data. Because ML and DL are sexier, these skills get more attention on the (social) media and people tend to believe that they are the right solution to any problem.
The result is that organizations ask for advanced knowledge in ML and/or DL when hiring Data Scientists (being a Kaggle champion, having a PhD...), and, because of that, Data Scientists tend to develop more on those skills.
The result many times is that the Data Scientists don't do the basic Business Understanding and Data Understanding and miss the opportunity to reach important conclusions with simples data analysis. At the same time, they get frustrated because the organizations rarely have the infrastructure or the data quality for them to apply the ML/DL knowledge that were demanded from them.

“The battle for #artificialintelligence supremacy is a binary game between US 🇺🇸 & China 🇨🇳”— this is what the media likes you to believe.

3) AI tech & tools 
are accessible to anyone with internet and hunger to solve a problem.

comments.text: AI in this post is misused in the discussion and misleads the audience. It is not the technical advacement in AI that ppl talk about growth, but the economic prosperity that accompanies it. No technology would be invested in unless there’s a huge ROI. In that sense, the trend is and will be more and more binary, which is unfortunate, simply because of economy of scale. But there still does exist chance for !US and !China countries to prosper with AI as the economy built on top of it will be more indigenous, given that nowadays AI rather innovates traditional local business.

Yeah, to some extent... innovation can come from everywhere but talent is scarce. Infrastructure and access to data is not democratized and USA and China are sitting on both. The same applies to AI and Tech tools mostly people using AI platforms from internet giants. Again yes, you can innovate, but can you scale fast enough outside USA or China? Support in Europe or elsewhere is not the same (yet) maybe things will change but will require a more eco-systematic approach.

comments.text:
There is one medium sized country that attracts the best AI talent from all over the world. One humongous country that grows and then retains most of its own talent, and just recently started asking an AI wizard or two to help out. There is another humongous country that grows talent, and then loses all of it. There is a large union of countries that will do anything in their power to limit AI innovative culture with constant and excessive regulatory pressure, and is not considered by anyone to be in the running. You also have one tiny country that accidentally developed a large international AI presence. You have another couple AI blips around the globe that are doing some interesting things.

I’ll leave it to you to figure out where your country fits in.

Application link: https://fb.me/GHCScholar
